title: Nature journal page design
subtitle: Laying out a nature journal double-page spread for field notes and observation
excerpt: A nature journal is part sketchbook, part field notebook. A well-designed page layout makes observations easy to record in the field and enjoyable to read later. This tutorial covers the basic double-page spread format: a combination of sketch area, written notes, and reference details that works whether you are sitting by a hedgerow or at a desk.
sourceNotes: Nature journal layout guidance synthesised from the field-notebook and nature-journalling traditions.

glossaryTerms.definition: Written observations made in situ — notes on location, time, weather, and behaviour recorded at the moment of observation rather than from memory. In a nature journal, field notes complement the visual records.

content.text: A nature journal is a personal record, not a technical document. The layout can be as loose or as structured as you prefer. The starting principle is that the visual record (sketch or watercolour) and the written record (species, date, conditions, observations) should sit together on the same spread, rather than being separated into a sketchbook and a written notebook.

A workable layout for most nature journal entries: the left-hand page is for the main sketch or watercolour observation, a plant, bird, insect, or landscape detail. The right-hand page carries the written

, with a small secondary sketch or detail study if needed. The date, location, time, and weather go in a consistent position on the right page, top right is the convention, so information is easy to find when scanning back through the journal.
Creating a colour palette for a spread

Before starting the watercolour sketch, mix the colours you will need and swatch them in a corner of the right page or along the gutter. A colour palette record is useful later for identifying plants or species from memory, and it adds visual coherence to the spread. Use a small strip of washi tape to mark the gutter so the spread stays together when the book is closed, a small practical detail in an A5 or A6 journal that gets a lot of use.

Field notes written in the field are better than notes written from memory later. Take the journal out. Write in pencil or a waterproof ink pen if the weather is uncertain. Leave space for details you will add later, a vernacular name, a Latin binomial, a grid reference. The notes do not need to be polished: fragmentary observations made on the spot are more valuable than a smooth account written at home.

A small pressed leaf, a feather, a seed, a photograph printed small, all add texture to a page. Attach with a loop of washi tape so the item can be lifted and re-examined. Do not use permanent adhesive for loose items: they benefit from being handled.
